food_name: Aloo Gobi (North Indian Potato and Cauliflower Dry Curry)
aliases: North Indian potato cauliflower dry curry
印度土豆花椰菜咖喱 (Yìndù tǔdòu huāyēcài gālí)

english: North Indian potato cauliflower dry curry
chinese: 印度土豆花椰菜咖喱 (Yìndù tǔdòu huāyēcài gālí)

food_subgroup: North Indian dry vegetable curry (sabzi) — potato (aloo) dominant carbohydrate with cauliflower (gobi)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for aloo gobi. Closest parent concept: potato and vegetable dry curry. FoodOn does not currently enumerate individual Indian regional sabzi preparations.
ontology.foodex2_description: Punjabi dry potato and cauliflower curry with Indian spices — closest parent match in FoodEx2; no exact code for aloo gobi exists
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0SAL is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a mixed vegetable curry preparation. No exact FoodEx2 code for aloo gobi.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for aloo gobi as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of aloo gobi is approximately 250g, comprising roughly 125g cubed potato and 100g cauliflower florets cooked in an onion-tomato-spice base with minimal oil, as typically served as a side dish in a North Indian thali or with roti.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Gobi — ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN 2017 Indian Food Composition Tables for aloo gobi and its component ingredients (potato, cauliflower, onion, tomato, cooking oil, spices). Values represent a composite per-100g profile for a standard North Indian home-style preparation with minimal oil. Minor trace minerals (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA SR Legacy component data where ICMR-NIN does not report them, at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — boiled potato (GI ~72) moderated by fat and acid co-ingestion
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (Atkinson et al., 2021)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for aloo gobi as a composite dish. GI estimated at ~62 using the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled potato (GI ~72) as the dominant carbohydrate source, moderated downward by: (1) fat coating of starch granules from cooking oil reducing starch gelatinisation rate, (2) organic acid contribution from tomato lowering gastric emptying rate, and (3) cauliflower (GI ~15) diluting the overall carbohydrate load. GL per 250g serving calculated as: (62 × 40g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.8, rounded to 23 per ICMR-NIN carbohydrate figure. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (~475mg per 250g serving) offset by high potassium (~875mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Aloo gobi provides approximately 475mg sodium per standard 250g serving — relatively low for an Indian curry — and its high potassium content (~875mg per serving from potato) supports a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; patients with hypertension may include it as a side dish provided overall daily sodium intake remains within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-high glycaemic load (GL ~23 per 250g serving) driven by potato starch (GI ~62)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycaemic load of approximately 23 per 250g serving is moderate-to-high for a single side dish; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 100–150g, pair with high-fibre accompaniments such as dal or whole-wheat roti rather than white rice, and note that cooling and reheating the dish increases resistant starch content, further moderating the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.5g per 100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, anti-inflammatory spice profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo gobi is a heart-favourable dish with very low saturated fat (1.25g per 250g serving), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and an anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, cumin, coriander) that may support endothelial health; patients with cardiovascular disease may consume it regularly as part of a balanced diet, with attention to total oil quantity used in preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, cumin) and high Vitamin C (~62mg per serving); anecdotal nightshade concern unsubstantiated
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory spice profile and high Vitamin C content (~62mg per 250g serving from cauliflower, tomato, and potato) are beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; anecdotal concerns about nightshade vegetables (potato) worsening RA symptoms have limited clinical evidence and should not preclude consumption in the absence of individual intolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Gobi is a quintessential North Indian (Punjabi) dry vegetable curry (sabzi) made from cubed potatoes (aloo) and cauliflower florets (gobi) slow-cooked in a spiced onion-tomato base with minimal oil. The dish is seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander powder, ginger, garlic, and green chilli, giving it a characteristic golden-yellow colour and earthy, mildly spiced flavour. It is finished with fresh coriander leaves and often a sprinkle of amchur (dried mango powder) for a tangy note. Texture is tender-crisp for the cauliflower and soft for the potato. A standard 250g serving provides approximately 275 kcal, 6g protein, 10g fat, and 40g carbohydrate. The dish is fully vegan, gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free. Glycaemic index is estimated at ~62 (medium) with a glycaemic load of ~23 per serving — moderate-to-high due to potato starch, moderated by fat, acid, and cauliflower dilution. Cauliflower contributes glucosinolates (sulforaphane precursors) and high Vitamin C (~62mg per serving). Cooling and reheating increases resistant starch. Nutritional data sourced from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017. Aloo Gobi is a staple home-cooked meal across North India and is widely served in Indian restaurants and thali meals in Singapore. It was notably featured in the British-Indian film 'Bend It Like Beckham' (2002).

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN 2017 Indian Food Composition Tables, the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Values are internally consistent and plausible for a minimal-oil dry potato-cauliflower preparation. Minor trace minerals (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA SR Legacy component data where ICMR-NIN does not report them, at confidence 0.9. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (boiled potato) with documented moderation rationale. Overall confidence is high.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (naturally zero for a fully plant-based vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients). Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero (naturally zero — this is a vegan dish with no animal products). Vitamin D is confirmed zero (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients present). Trans fat is confirmed zero (naturally zero — no hydrogenated oils used in traditional preparation). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by ICMR-NIN for this composite dish; set to -1. Water content not reported for this composite preparation; set to -1. GL per serving calculated as (GI 62 × 40g available carbohydrate per 250g serving) / 100 = 24.8; input record states 23.0 — minor rounding difference retained from source. FoodOn URI not available for aloo gobi; closest parent used. SNOMED CT code not available. FoodEx2 code A0SAL used as closest defensible parent. Punjabi alias (ਆਲੂ ਗੋਭੀ) included in aliases_by_language under a dedicated punjabi key as it is a primary regional language for this dish. Q8 fix (iteration 3): all aliases_by_language entries — including '印度土豆花椰菜咖喱 (Yìndù tǔdòu huāyēcài gālí)', 'आलू गोभी (Aloo Gobi)', and 'ਆਲੂ ਗੋਭੀ' — are now explicitly present in the flat aliases array, ensuring full consistency between the flat array and the structured object.
